response:
**Results**

We evaluated ten experimental variants of our proposed method on the validation set, reporting validation accuracy as the mean ± standard deviation over five random seeds for each configuration. Table 1 summarizes the performance of all variants.

The baseline configuration (variant_03), which excludes the proposed component, achieved a validation accuracy of 67.8 ± 0.6. Among the nine variants incorporating the proposed component, only variant_08 outperformed the baseline, attaining a validation accuracy of 70.4 ± 0.5. This represents a substantial absolute gain of +2.6 percentage points over the baseline, indicating that, under the specific settings of variant_08, our proposed component can yield significant improvements.

However, the remaining eight variants with the proposed component (variants 01, 02, 04, 05, 06, 07, 09, and 10) did not outperform the baseline. Their accuracies ranged from 64.7 ± 0.7 (variant_06) to 67.2 ± 0.8 (variant_01), all falling short of the baseline. The closest to the baseline was variant_01, with a marginal difference of -0.6 absolute points, while the majority of the variants exhibited decreases of 1–3 percentage points.

These results suggest that the effectiveness of the proposed component is highly sensitive to configuration choices. While most variants yielded either comparable or lower accuracy compared to the baseline, the configuration used in variant_08 demonstrates that the proposed method has the potential to improve performance when appropriately tuned. Further analysis is warranted to identify the factors contributing to the superior performance of variant_08 and to generalize these findings.

In summary, only a single experimental configuration (variant_08) outperformed the baseline, while the majority of proposed configurations did not, highlighting the importance of careful hyperparameter selection and ablation studies.
